texts.full_text: ent. All ten patients with adenocarcinoma limited to the mucosa (pT1a) were alive at 5 years; of 31 with submucosal infiltration (pT1b) 79 per cent survived to 5 years (P not significant). Patients without lymph node metastasis had a 5-year survival rate of 81 per cent, compared with 50 per cent for those in the pN1 category (P not significant). Oesophagectomy for early oesophageal adenocarcinoma is safe and leads to a favourable

texts.full_text: A: All of the forms of replace in std::string take positions, so you have to find the existing string,Certainly! To achieve what you're looking for—replacing all occurrences of a substring within another string—you'll need to repeatedly search and remove that specific pattern. The standard `std::replace` function does not directly support replacing substrings with an empty string based on content; it works more like filling in ranges by position. Here's how you can create your own replacement loop using the C++ Standard Library: ```cpp #include <iostream> #include <string> void replaceSubstring(std::string &str, const std::string& from, const std::string& to) { if (from.empty()) return; size_t start_pos = 0; while ((start_pos = str.find(from, start_pos)) != std::string::npos) { // Find the first occurrence str.replace(start_pos, from.length(), to); // Replace it with 'to' if (from.empty()) break; // Avoid infinite loop in case of empty substring start_pos += to.size(); // Move past this replacement and look for next one. } } int main() { std::string gg = "13332"; replaceSubstring(gg, "3", ""); // This will remove all '3's from the string std::cout << gg; // Outputs: 12 } ``` In

texts.full_text: mation from different studies. - Model-Based Optimal Design: This approach uses models to optimize the design of clinical studies. To ensure the optimized use of these models, the following scientific rules and thoughts should be followed: - Model Selection: The choice of the model should be based on the scientific question, the data, and the underlying assumptions. - Model Qualification: The model should be evaluated for its predictive and descriptive performance. - Model Application: The model should be applied to the scientific question, and the limitations of the model should be acknowledged. - Model Revision: The model should be revised and updated as new data and information become available. In conclusion, the optimization of methods and models in clinical pharmacology is an iterative and rigorous scientific process
